name: Hermes HIR
summary: Meta's Hermes JS engine lowers JS to a high-level IR before emitting Hermes Bytecode. SSA-form with JS-aware operations that retain enough source semantics — closures, lexical scopes, hoisting, arguments materialization, try/catch regions — for the optimizer to reason about them.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Meta's Hermes JavaScript engine lowers JS to a high-level IR (HIR) before emitting Hermes Bytecode (HBC). HIR is SSA-form with JS-aware operations that retain enough source semantics — closures, lexical scopes, hoisting, `arguments` materialization, `try`/`catch` regions — for the optimizer to reason about them before they are flattened into register-based bytecode.

sections.title: Scope and closure analysis at HIR level
sections.content: Two HIR-level details worth recording. First, HIR carries enough scope information to decide *which captured variables can become stack slots* rather than living on a heap-allocated `Environment` — the Hermes-specific equivalent of escape analysis on closures. Second, HIR is what makes `Function.prototype.toString` and similar JS reflective features survivable across optimization: the bytecode emitter consults HIR-level annotations to recover source-faithful behaviour for the few APIs that demand it.

sections.title: Build-time HIR, ship-only HBC
sections.content:
HIR runs at app build time, not on device. Hermes precompiles JS to HIR, optimises HIR (eager type narrowing, dead-code elimination, scope flattening, stack-promotion of captured variables), then lowers to HBC, which is what ships on the device. There is no JIT and HIR is not present at runtime — the device only sees HBC.

This split is what makes the Hermes pipeline unusual: the HIR is throwaway optimisation infrastructure consumed entirely at build time, while the bytecode is the durable artefact.
